does: This paper identifies and empirically demonstrates a critical stochasticity problem in LLM jailbreak research: advertised attack success rates (ASR) are unstable and unreliable metrics that systematically inflate reported performance. The authors show that jailbreak methods from reputable institutions exhibit dramatic performance variability—a prompt achieving 80% ASR in published evaluations may succeed only 50% of the time when tested repeatedly on the same model. Through systematic analysis, they document that ASR measurements are not stable quantities across runs, target models, or evaluation conditions, making published comparisons between different jailbreak techniques fundamentally unreliable. This stochasticity undermines the validity of claimed improvements and prevents accurate assessment of defense effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of adversarial attacks against language models, extending prior jailbreak research from groups like Anthropic (BoN) and Microsoft Research (Crescendo) that established ASR as the standard evaluation metric. It responds to and critiques the evaluation methodology underlying recent prominent papers in adversarial prompting and red-teaming, which have increasingly focused on achieving high ASR scores as markers of success. The paper connects to broader concerns about reproducibility and metric stability in ML research, engaging with foundational questions about whether published benchmarks accurately reflect real-world performance and whether results generalize across different model implementations and defense mechanisms.
enables: This work points toward fundamental reforms in LLM jailbreak evaluation methodology, suggesting the field needs more robust, stable metrics and reporting practices that account for stochasticity. It opens directions for developing variance-aware evaluation protocols, confidence intervals for ASR estimates, and standardized testing procedures that prevent metric inflation. The findings motivate research into understanding the sources of jailbreak instability—whether arising from model sampling, prompt variation, or defense mechanisms—and could enable better red-teaming practices. More broadly, this work could catalyze methodological improvements across AI safety evaluation, encouraging the field to move beyond single-point performance estimates toward more rigorous statistical characterization of attack robustness.
